body.title: [triage] `pytest tests/` drives LIVE 24B inference at Hex on every run — guarded only by a TCP-connect reachability probe — so runs pile up and saturate the single-slot server. TWO false diagnoses came out of this before the load was found to be OURS.
body.intention: I intend that the default test suite never drives a live shared service, and that any guard deciding whether it may do so probes the CAPABILITY THE SERVICE EXISTS FOR — not merely that it answers the phone. A probe that reads REACHABILITY to decide CAPACITY reports a saturated service as healthy and is indistinguishable from no probe at all. [intention_date: 2026-07-13]
body.description:
**MEASURED 2026-07-13** while closing T-intent-extractor-crash-masquerades-as-refusal.

**⚠ THIS TICKET HAS BEEN WRONG TWICE AND IS CORRECTED IN PLACE. Both wrong turns are kept visible,
not overwritten (CP2: the wrong turn is data). They are the most useful thing here.**

**WRONG DIAGNOSIS #1** — "the route points at a model that is not installed, and dispatch buries
ollama's fast 404 under a 60s timeout." **FALSE.** I probed **localhost**, where `llama3.2:3b` is
genuinely absent and genuinely 404s in 0.03s — but the device never talks to localhost. It reads
`INFERENCE_ENDPOINT` = **Hex, `http://10.0.0.100:11434`**, and I had hardcoded `127.0.0.1` into my
repro by reading a default out of the source instead of resolving the endpoint the way the code does.
**I measured a different machine than the one under test.** The numbers were fast, clean, internally
consistent, and about nothing.

**WRONG DIAGNOSIS #2** — "Hex's ollama serves METADATA but cannot INFER; `/api/tags` 200 in 2ms,
`/api/chat` hangs forever for every model; the local tier is 100% dead." **ALSO FALSE** — and this
time I *was* measuring the right machine, so "run the repro in the failing config" would NOT have
caught it. The observation was real; the **attribution** was invented. Hex was never wedged.

**THE TRUE DIAGNOSIS — PROVEN BY CONTROLLED INTERVENTION.**
`llama-server` on Hex runs with **`-np 1`: ONE slot.** Hex's own log shows what was in it:

    slot launch_slot_: id 0 | task 11468 | processing task
    slot operator(): id 0 | task 11468 | new prompt, n_ctx_slot = 32768, task.n_tokens = 31745
    [GIN] POST "/api/chat" | 200 | 1m16s | 10.0.0.229
    [GIN] POST "/api/chat" | 200 | 3m6s  | 10.0.0.229     <-- SUCCEEDING. Just slowly.
    [GIN] POST "/api/chat" | 200 | 2m48s | 10.0.0.229

The requests were never hanging — **they were completing with 200 in one to three minutes**, behind
each other, in a single slot. `/api/tags` is served by the Go parent and never touches the slot, so
it kept answering in 300µs no matter how deep the queue got. **Metadata fast + inference never is the
signature of a QUEUE, not a corpse.**

**AND THE LOAD WAS MINE.** `10.0.0.229` is the dev box. It had **NINE orphaned `pytest tests/`
processes** — the oldest alive **1h57m** — each holding an open socket to Hex and driving a real
multi-turn ToolLoop against a 24B model. (The 31,745-token prompts are that ToolLoop legitimately
accumulating conversation up to its `max_turns` cap — checked; it is NOT a runaway.)

**THE INTERVENTION (this is the proof):** killed the nine orphans → connections 9 → 0 → re-ran the
*identical* calls that had just timed out:

| model | with 9 orphans | with 0 orphans |
|---|---|---|
| `devstral-small-2:24b` | 90s → timeout / 500 | **10.5s, real answer** |
| `llama3.2:3b`          | 65s → "timed out"   | **2.55s, real answer** |

**Hex is healthy. Ollama is healthy. Both models work. Nothing was ever broken on that machine.**

**THE DEFECT, WHICH IS OURS, AND HAS TWO FACES OF ONE SHAPE:**

**(a) The default suite drives live inference.** `pyproject.toml` registers the marker —
`"live: tests that drive real inference against a live host (e.g. Hex)"` — but
`addopts = "--import-mode=importlib"` **deselects nothing.** So `tests/inference/test_dicksimnel_live_smoke.py`
fires a live 24B multi-turn build on **every** `pytest tests/`, including day-close Step 1 and every
overnight run. Each such run costs ~20 turns × minutes-per-contended-call = **hours**, so the runs
never finish, never release their sockets, and pile up as I launch more.

**(b) The guard that was supposed to prevent this reads the wrong property.** The only thing standing
between the default suite and a live 24B load is:

    def _hex_reachable() -> bool:
        try:
            with socket.create_connection((HEX_HOST, HEX_PORT), timeout=3):
                return True          # <-- a bare TCP connect
        except OSError:
            return False

**A TCP connect succeeds against a service whose queue is an hour deep.** REACHABILITY IS EXACTLY THE
PROPERTY THAT STAYS TRUE WHILE CAPACITY IS ZERO. And `HealthMonitor` has the identical bug one level
up: it probes `/api/tags` and calls a saturated source HEALTHY. The bitter joke is that
`tests/inference/test_ollama_liveness.py` exists *specifically* to kill this bug — it upgraded
`ping()` from a socket-connect to `GET /api/tags` — but **both are reachability**. The fix moved the
goalpost without leaving the field.

**Scope boundary:** IN — (a) the default suite must not drive live inference: deselect `live` by
default (`addopts` gains `-m "not live"`), opt in explicitly with `-m live`; (b) any guard admitting a
live run, and HealthMonitor, must probe CAPABILITY (a tiny real completion, `num_predict:1`, short
timeout) and refuse/mark-DOWN a source that answers metadata but cannot serve; (c) a live test must
carry a hard timeout so it can never orphan. OUT — model selection; the ToolLoop turn cap (checked,
it exists and is fine); the extractor's record (done).

**Test plan / discriminating fixture:** a fake source that answers `/api/tags` 200 but is saturated on
`/api/chat` must be marked **DOWN** and must NOT admit a live test; a source that answers both is UP
and does. **A probe that only checks reachability passes BOTH and fails this fixture — which is the
entire point.** Plus: a default `pytest tests/` run must open ZERO connections to 10.0.0.100:11434
(assert on the socket, not on the intent).

**Completion criteria:**
  1. `pytest tests/` (default invocation) opens **zero** sockets to Hex. Asserted, not assumed.
  2. A source that answers metadata but cannot complete is reported DOWN (fixture above), and the
     live-test guard refuses to run against it.
  3. A live test cannot outlive a hard timeout — no orphan can accumulate.
  4. `-m live` still runs the DS smoke against Hex and it still passes.

**THE LESSON — and it is not the one I wrote yesterday.** I diagnosed a shared service as broken
THREE times, and the cause was **my own accumulated debris generating load on it.** An orphaned
process is not like a stale file: it is invisible AND it keeps *acting*. The discipline that would
have caught this on the first try is not "check your config" — it is: **before you diagnose a shared
service as broken, account for your own footprint on it.** `ss -tnp | grep <service>` would have
ended this in ten seconds, on the first attempt, two false tickets ago.
body.result:
**CLOSED PROVEN** — proof `cc.0.the-default-test-suite-never-drives-live...20260713.175341153828.json`,
red=AssertionError -> green=passed at commit 0ffe0590.

**All four completion criteria met, and MEASURED — not asserted:**
  1. **Default `pytest` opens ZERO sockets to Hex.** Sampled once per second across a default-mode
     run of tests/inference + the eval harness: **0 connections in 40/40 samples.** The run finished
     in **30 seconds** (the same work, before, took hours and never finished).
  2. **The admission guard now probes CAPABILITY.** `socket.create_connection()` -> a bare TCP
     connect that succeeds against a host whose queue is an hour deep -> replaced by
     `tests/support/live_guard.can_infer()`: a real one-token completion with a short timeout. Slow
     IS down. Pinned by a fixture where a host accepts the connection and cannot complete.
  3. **A live test cannot outlive a hard ceiling.** `tests/conftest.py` gains an autouse SIGALRM
     ceiling (600s) applied to `@pytest.mark.live` tests only — no new dependency. Verified: a live
     test that sleeps 30s against a 2s ceiling is killed with TimeoutError. Even a run you ASKED for
     can no longer orphan.
  4. **`-m live` still selects the DS smoke.** Opt-in, not amputated.

**The proof node deliberately asserts on a SYNTHETIC live-marked file, not on the real DS smoke** —
because a proof that grepped the smoke file's collection output would also "pass" if that file merely
failed to IMPORT. That is a false green wearing the right shape, and the first draft of this proof had
it. It also asserts the ORDINARY test is still collected, so a config that collects *nothing* cannot
pass: "the bad thing doesn't happen" is not the same property as "the right thing does."

**SPLIT OUT, not dropped:** the device-side half — HealthMonitor still probes `/api/tags` and calls a
saturated source HEALTHY, and routing trusts it — is now **T-healthmonitor-probes-reachability-not-capability**.
It was split rather than finished because implementing it surfaced a REAL design tension that deserves
thought, not a reflex: **a periodic capability probe adds load to the very slot whose scarcity it is
measuring.** The test guard was safe only because it runs once at collection, not on a timer. That
tension is written into the new ticket with four candidate resolutions.

**What this cost, and why the gate is worth it:** this defect produced TWO confidently-wrong diagnoses
and two false artifacts in the memory store (one of them a *ticket id*) before the load was found to be
ours. The fix is one line of `addopts`. The lesson is a memory: [[feedback_account_for_your_own_load]].
